blocks.text:
There is no information on population size or trend for this plant; it is known from only three herbarium specimen collections dating from 1958 to 1974, corresponding to three widely separated localities stretching from southern to northern Mozambique. It is recorded from lowland woodland and forest habitats including Brachystegia (Miombo) woodland. At an estimated 48,214 km2 the extent of occurrence (EOO) is well above the threshold for a threatened category, and the actual area of occupancy (AOO) is likely to be much higher than the minimum (12 km2) currently estimated based on known records, given the large EOO and the large areas of Miombo woodland habitat remaining in Mozambique.

The main threat to this species is habitat loss and degradation, due to demand for natural resources such as charcoal and timber, expansion of agricultural land and settlements, cattle grazing and potentially invasive plant species. Although there is an observed continuing decline in the area and quality of habitat, particularly gallery forest and Mecrusse forest, currently only one of the three recorded locations - Goba - appears to face any significant threats. It is plausible that in the future similar threats could also significantly impact the locations in Inhambane Province (Mabote District, 27 km west of lake Banamana) and Cabo Delgado Province (30 km north of Nantulo). These impacts are unlikely to drive this species to quickly become Critically Endangered or Extinct, especially as is suspected that the species may occur at additional sites, but such threats could result in the species qualifying as Endangered. Therefore, this species is assessed as Near Threatened under criterion D2.


Research is needed on its current population size, distribution and trends, and on threats to any additional areas where it occurs.

The main threat to this species is habitat loss and degradation, due to demand for natural resources such as charcoal and timber, expansion of agricultural land and settlements, cattle grazing and potentially invasive plant species. Although there is an observed continuing decline in the area and quality of habitat, particularly gallery forest and Mecrusse forest, currently only one of the three recorded locations - Goba - appears to face any significant threats. It is plausible that in the future similar threats could also significantly impact the locations in Inhambane Province (Mabote District, 27 km west of lake Banamana) and Cabo Delgado Province (30 km north of Nantulo). These impacts are unlikely to drive this species to quickly become Critically Endangered or Extinct, especially as is suspected that the species may occur at additional sites, but such threats could result in the species qualifying as Endangered. Therefore, this species is assessed as Near Threatened under criterion D2.


Research is needed on its current population size, distribution and trends, and on threats to any additional areas where it occurs.

blocks.text: This plant has been recorded mainly in Mozambique, where it is known from three widely separated localities stretching from southern to northern Mozambique: Goba in the Lebombo Mountains in Maputo Province; Mabote District in Inhambane Province; and Montepuez District in Cabo Delgado Province. There is also an unconfirmed historical report from adjacent Eswatini near the southern border (van der Merwe 2016). It has an estimated extent of occurrence (EOO) of 48,214 km2. The minimum area of occupancy (AOO) based on known records is 12 km2 - the actual AOO is likely to be much higher given the large EOO and the large areas of Miombo woodland habitat remaining in Mozambique. It is recorded from three different habitats in inland areas of Mozambique, at about 100-400 m asl.

There is also an unconfirmed historical report from adjacent Eswatini near the southern border (van der Merwe 2016). It has an estimated extent of occurrence (EOO) of 48,214 km2. The minimum area of occupancy (AOO) based on known records is 12 km2 - the actual AOO is likely to be much higher given the large EOO and the large areas of Miombo woodland habitat remaining in Mozambique. It is recorded from three different habitats in inland areas of Mozambique, at about 100-400 m asl.

The sites in Mabote and Montepuez are not currently thought to be under threat, though plausible future threats are presented by mining concessions.

blocks.text: No population data are available for this species and the current population trend is unknown. It is known from only three herbarium specimen collections dating from 1958 to 1974. A reconnaissance expedition to the Lebombo Mountains in March 2018 (Osborne 2018) did not find this species near Goba, although survey work and collecting was very limited on this short trip. Field work in Mabote District in February 2019 (Osborne et al. 2019) also did not locate this species in Miombo woodland or Mecrusse forest, although the survey did not extend as far west as this species was recorded. Despite this lack of evidence, the species is probably locally common in intact woodland.

Field work in Mabote District in February 2019 (Osborne et al. 2019) also did not locate this species in Miombo woodland or Mecrusse forest, although the survey did not extend as far west as this species was recorded. Despite this lack of evidence, the species is probably locally common in intact woodland.

blocks.text: This member of the sweet potato family is a twining or climbing herb or liana with white flowers. It is recorded from three different habitats in inland areas of Mozambique: Androstachys johnsonii (Mecrusse or Lebombo-ironwood) forest near Goba in the Lebombo mountains; Brachystegia (Miombo) woodland on sandy soil in Inhambane Province, and in dense gallery forest on reddish sandy-clay soil in Cabo Delgado Province. It has also been reported from seasonally flooded grassland margins near drainage lines [Goncalves 1987].
blocks.text_rich: This member of the sweet potato family is a twining or climbing herb or liana with white flowers. It is recorded from three different habitats in inland areas of Mozambique: Androstachys johnsonii (Mecrusse or Lebombo-ironwood) forest near Goba in the Lebombo mountains; Brachystegia (Miombo) woodland on sandy soil in Inhambane Province, and in dense gallery forest on reddish sandy-clay soil in Cabo Delgado Province. It has also been reported from seasonally flooded grassland margins near drainage lines [Goncalves 1987].

blocks.text:
The main threat to this species is habitat loss and degradation. In the Goba area, Mecrusse forest is threatened by logging for timber and charcoal, and the need for land for subsistence agriculture (machamba agriculture including maize and cassava) driven by population growth and the spread of rural communities. Several non-native and potentially invasive species were recorded during the 2018 survey (Osborne 2018), including Lantana camara, Agave sisalana, Opuntia ficus-indica, and Zinnia peruviana. Cattle grazing poses an additional threat to vegetation. The Mozambican moon-creeper may also be affected by roadside maintenance and widening of access tracks, although this threat has not been quantified.

Currently, the locations in Inhambane Province (Mabote District, 27 km west of lake Banamana) and Cabo Delgado Province (30 km north of Nantulo) look relatively unimpacted, particularly the latter location, where settlement and habitat clearance for agriculture is limited and there are extensive areas of intact gallery forest and woodland (Google Earth 2021). In Mabote District, Mecrusse forest is under threat from logging for timber and charcoal and clearance for agriculture, and Androstachys seedlings and regeneration were not seen in cleared areas (Osborne et al. 2019). Miombo woodland is threatened by agricultural and settlement expansion, but is more extensive and does seem to regenerate (Osborne et al. 2019).

The location west of lake Banamana falls within a Sasol gas exploration concession, and the area north of Nantulo is covered by several mining exploration concessions for gemstones, gold, graphite and stone for construction (MIREM and Trimble Land Administration 2021). In the longer term, it is possible that these industries and related infrastructure development coupled with ongoing population growth will lead to migration of people into these areas, increasing impacts on the forest and woodland habitats due to increased demand for natural resources such as charcoal and timber, and expansion of settlements and agricultural land.

Currently, the locations in Inhambane Province (Mabote District, 27 km west of lake Banamana) and Cabo Delgado Province (30 km north of Nantulo) look relatively unimpacted, particularly the latter location, where settlement and habitat clearance for agriculture is limited and there are extensive areas of intact gallery forest and woodland (Google Earth 2021). In the longer term, it is possible that these industries and related infrastructure development coupled with ongoing population growth will lead to migration of people into these areas, increasing impacts on the forest and woodland habitats due to increased demand for natural resources such as charcoal and timber, and expansion of settlements and agricultural land.

blocks.text:
No specific conservation measures or management plan are in place for this species. It is not recorded from any protected areas and is not known to be represented in any ex situ collections (BGCI 2021). A section of the Mozambique part of the Lebombo Mountains is being considered as a Tropical Important Plant Area (Darbyshire et al. 2019). Seed banking and roadside firebreak maintenance are likely to be the most urgent conservation needs.

Research is needed on its current population size, distribution and trends, and on threats to the areas where it occurs.
